text.title: tool-migration-process Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for tool-migration-process. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Complete Phase 1 Planning with explicit stakeholder alignment, tool selection or evaluation, scope definition, timeline with 50% buffer over measured dry-run time, and risk assessment.
rule.rationale: Skipping planning or condensing it into a few days causes scope creep, misaligned expectations, and timeline overruns that compound through subsequent phases.

rule.statement: Freeze the field mapping specification at end of Preparation; mid-migration mapping changes silently double scope.
rule.rationale: Each mapping change requires re-transforming and re-validating already-migrated records.

rule.statement: Keep the source tool read-only for at least 30 days post-cutover; never delete it during stabilization.
rule.rationale: Teams reference historical context from source; premature deletion forces re-migration of data that was intentionally excluded.

rule.statement: Budget 2x the planned stabilization window; it always overruns.
rule.rationale: Edge cases and user adaptation issues accumulate in week 3-4 after initial excitement fades.

rule.statement: Design the migration engine as a CLI tool the agent invokes; do not run migrations as inline LLM calls.
rule.rationale: Long-running migrations exceed agent token budgets; the CLI pattern keeps the agent as orchestrator with human-reviewable artifacts at each checkpoint.

rule.statement: Run a full dry-run on production data into a sandbox target one week before cutover; measure exact runtime then add 50% buffer.
rule.rationale: Rate limits routinely double planned cutover downtime; dry-run measurement is the only reliable source of timing data.

rule.statement: Freeze the field mapping specification before the first dry-run; forbid runtime mapping mutations.
rule.rationale: Each mapping change requires re-transforming already-processed records; LLMs that "fix" mappings on the fly silently corrupt the ID mapping and cause orphaned links.

rule.statement: Force-list the 0.5% of failed records; they are always the most-linked tickets.
rule.rationale: A 99.5% success rate looks fine in aggregate but the missing records are disproportionately high-value due to cross-reference density.

rule.statement: Validate three numbers before declaring any wave or cutover successful: issue count, comment count, and attachment count by byte.
rule.rationale: Issue count alone misses corrupted comments and missing attachments; the three-number check catches the most common silent data-loss patterns.

rule.statement: Cutover on a low-traffic window (Friday afternoon if org tolerates weekend quiet); never mid-week for large migrations.
rule.rationale: Weekend windows provide recovery time before full business-hours load resumes; mid-week cutovers interrupt peak productivity with no buffer.

rule.statement: Pre-write rollback communications before cutover begins so they can be sent within seconds of a decision to revert.
rule.rationale: Rollback decisions under stress are slowed by the need to draft communications; pre-writing removes this bottleneck and enables faster response.

rule.statement: Rollback only works if no critical data was created exclusively in the target during cutover; a single shipped feature with target-only context can pin you.
rule.rationale: Once teams start creating tickets in the new tool, those records don't exist in the source; rollback would lose that work, making true revert impossible.

rule.statement: Rate-limit webhook-based notifications to one summary per migration phase; do not send a message per event.
rule.rationale: Retry storms in migration engines can trigger hundreds of webhook notifications, spamming channels and causing teams to mute the notifications needed for real alerts.
